hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — 9, 10. The table saw a raise, which both you and your opponent called, setting the stage for a potentially explosive hand.
Flop — 9, 7, 8. You decided to bet, but your opponent swiftly raised, indicating they might have caught something.
Turn — 8. Your opponent took the initiative with a bet, and you chose to call, trying to gauge their strength.
River — 5. With the board now complete, your opponent fired again — half the pot, putting the pressure on you to make a tough decision.

wrongReasons: 10 6 has similar strength, but look at when the aggression shifted — the turn card connects with the correct hand in a way it doesn't connect with 10 6.
8 5 makes a full house here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
A 2 doesn't benefit from the turn card the way the correct hand does. A player holding only one pair wouldn't have the confidence to raise on that street.
hand.explanation:
Answer: 6 4

Your opponent's hand improved significantly on the turn with the 8 pairing one of their hole cards, and then they hit a straight on the river with the 5, utilizing their 6 and 4 to make a strong hand. This consistent betting pattern after the flop suggests they were value betting their made hand, particularly after improving on the turn and river.
